candidate_sources.title: Managing Alternaria Leaf Blight on Cantaloupe
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms of Alternaria leaf blight first appear as small tan circular spots with a white center on the upper leaf surface. As the disease progresses, the spots will enlarge and appear light brown. Spots darken with age and may show ridges, giving a target-like appearance. Alternaria leaf blight can cause infected plants to defoliate heavily, resulting in sunburn injury to the developing fruits. Caused by Alternaria cucumerina.

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbit Alternaria Leaf Blight - UW Vegetable Pathology
candidate_sources.snippet: Alternaria leaf blight is caused by the fungus Alternaria cucumerina and can rapidly defoliate plants, causing reductions in bulk yield. Alternaria leaf blight is a much greater threat to muskmelons than watermelons. Symptoms first appear at the time of fruit set on older leaves, beginning as small, necrotic spots that may be surrounded by a yellow halo. Lesions expand to form brown spots; a concentric ring pattern may be present if conditions are wet.

candidate_sources.title: Cantaloupe (Cucumis melo)-Alternaria Leaf Blight | Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks
candidate_sources.snippet: Lesions begin as small, tan or brown spots that may appear watersoaked on the underside of leaves. Alternaria infections occur only on leaves. Petioles, stems, and fruit are not directly affected by the pathogen. However, melons on defoliated vines ripen prematurely and result in lower quality fruit. At least 18 hours of high relative humidity, producing leaf wetness, is required before infection can occur.

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbits, Alternaria Leaf Spot - UMass Amherst Center for Agriculture
candidate_sources.snippet: Alternaria leaf spot/blight on cucurbits is caused by Alternaria cucumerina. Lesions expand to form brown spots ranging from 1/2 inch to 3/4 inch in size; a concentric ring pattern may be present if conditions are wet, and as lesions expand, they merge to form large, blighted areas, followed by leaf curling and eventual leaf drop. Management includes 3-4 year crop rotation and protectant fungicides beginning at fruit set.

candidate_sources.title: Anthracnose of Cucurbits | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Anthracnose damage on melon fruits, leaves and stems is caused by a fungus. Most cultivars of honeydew melon are very susceptible and this disease is a limiting factor in melon production in the eastern United States. Caused by Colletotrichum species; spots with pale yellow halos on leaves, sunken yellowish spots on fruits, dark brown spots on stems.

candidate_sources.title: Anthracnose of cucurbits | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Anthracnose of cucurbits is caused by Colletotrichum orbiculare and is destructive on cucumber, melon, and watermelon. Symptoms on melon include water-soaked spots that turn brown, leaf blighting, and reddish gum exudation on fruits. Favored by warm temperatures (74-78 F) and high humidity. Management includes certified seed, tolerant varieties, crop rotation, and fungicides such as chlorothalonil and mancozeb.

candidate_sources.title: Fact sheet - Cucurbit anthracnose (200) - Pacific Pests and Pathogens
candidate_sources.snippet: Cucurbit anthracnose caused by Colletotrichum orbiculare is a destructive disease that occurs in wet and warm weather, especially serious on cucumber, melon and watermelon. On melon, symptoms include greyish water-soaked spots and brown older spots that join together to form extensive rots. Infections often exude a reddish gum. Cultural and chemical management options are described.

candidate_sources.title: Colletotrichum orbiculare (anthracnose of cucurbits) - Bugwoodwiki
candidate_sources.snippet: Colletotrichum orbiculare causes anthracnose of cucurbits including cucumber, watermelon, and muskmelon (melon). The article covers host range, symptoms, biology, epidemiology, and management of the pathogen.

candidate_sources.title: Watermelon Bacterial Fruit Blotch | MU Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: The causal organism, Acidovorax avenae subsp. citrulli, was first identified in the United States in 1989 and in Missouri in 1994. Infections that develop early can destroy up to 100 percent of the marketable watermelon crop. Water-soaked, 'oily' areas on the underside of the cotyledons or seedling leaves often paralleling the veins with a yellow halo are characteristic symptoms.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial fruit blotch of cucurbits
candidate_sources.snippet: APS Education Center lesson on bacterial fruit blotch of cucurbits caused by Acidovorax citrulli, covering symptoms, disease cycle, epidemiology, and management on watermelon, melon, and other cucurbits.

candidate_sources.title: Watermelon (Citrullus sp.)-Bacterial Fruit Blotch/Seedling Blight | Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks
candidate_sources.snippet: Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbook entry covering Bacterial Fruit Blotch / Seedling Blight caused by Acidovorax citrulli on cucurbits, with symptoms, cultural and chemical control recommendations.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Fruit Blotch - Texas A&M Vegetable Resources
candidate_sources.snippet: Texas A&M Aggie Horticulture page on Bacterial Fruit Blotch caused by Acidovorax avenae subsp. citrulli in cucurbits, including symptoms on seedlings, foliage, and fruit and management practices.

candidate_sources.title: Acidovorax citrulli (bacterial fruit blotch) | CABI Compendium
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI Compendium datasheet on Acidovorax citrulli, the bacterial fruit blotch pathogen of cucurbits including watermelon and melon; covers distribution, hosts, symptoms, biology, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Acidovorax citrulli: generating basic and applied knowledge to tackle a global threat to the cucurbit industry - PMC
candidate_sources.snippet: Peer-reviewed review covering the biology, epidemiology, host range (watermelon, melon, cucumber), and management strategies for Acidovorax citrulli, causal agent of bacterial fruit blotch of cucurbits.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial wilt of cucurbits | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial wilt is a serious disease caused by Erwinia tracheiphila that can severely affect cantaloupe, muskmelon, and cucumbers. The bacteria overwinter in the gut of striped and spotted cucumber beetles. Bacterial wilt cannot be controlled by bactericides directly but is managed by controlling striped cucumber beetle.

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbits, Bacterial Wilt : Vegetable : Center for Agriculture, Food, and the Environment (CAFE) at UMass Amherst
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial wilt of cucurbits caused by Erwinia tracheiphila is vectored by striped and spotted cucumber beetles. An easy way to diagnose is to sever a runner close to the crown and slowly pull the cut surfaces apart - sticky strands will be visible connecting the cut surfaces if the pathogen is present.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Wilt of Cucurbits | Plant Problems | Illinois Extension | UIUC
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial wilt of cucurbits is caused by Erwinia tracheiphila and affects cucumbers, muskmelons, and to a lesser extent squash and pumpkins. Leaves first appear dull green, wilt during the day, recover at night, and eventually yellow and brown at the margins, leading to complete withering and death. The bacterium produces extracellular polysaccharides and blocks fluid flow in the xylem.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Wilt of Cucurbits - Wisconsin Horticulture
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial wilt of cucurbits, caused by Erwinia tracheiphila, is a devastating disease of cucumbers and muskmelons. Vectored by striped and spotted cucumber beetles. Management focuses on controlling beetle vectors via row covers, perimeter trap crops of Blue Hubbard squash, and insecticides especially before the 5-leaf stage.

candidate_sources.title: Charcoal Rot / Cucurbits / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: UC IPM Pest Management Guidelines for cucurbits covering charcoal rot caused by Macrophomina phaseolina, including symptoms, disease cycle and management on melon and other cucurbits.

candidate_sources.title: A Severe Outbreak of Charcoal Rot in Cantaloupe Melon Caused by Macrophomina phaseolina in Chile | Plant Disease
candidate_sources.snippet: APS Plant Disease report describing a severe outbreak of charcoal rot in cantaloupe melon caused by Macrophomina phaseolina, with symptom descriptions including wilting, leaf browning, water-soaked crown lesions with amber to dark brown exudates, and reddish fruit decay.

candidate_sources.title: Charcoal rot (Macrophomina phaseolina) across melon diversity: evaluating the interaction between the pathogen, plant age and environmental conditions as a step towards breeding for resistance
candidate_sources.snippet: European Journal of Plant Pathology peer-reviewed article evaluating charcoal rot (Macrophomina phaseolina) across melon germplasm diversity, including the interaction between pathogen, plant age, and environmental conditions, as a step toward breeding for resistance.

candidate_sources.title: Macrophomina phaseolina - Wikipedia
candidate_sources.snippet: Overview of Macrophomina phaseolina, a soilborne fungus causing charcoal rot in a wide range of hosts including melon, with information on sclerotia, host range (500+ species), survival in soil for 3-12 years, and stress-related disease development under high temperatures.

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbit Downy Mildew | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Cucurbit downy mildew is caused by the fungus-like oomycete pathogen Pseudoperonospora cubensis. Downy mildew causes angular, yellow to brown lesions on top of the leaves that are restricted by leaf veins. However, in melon, foliar lesions are less defined than those on cucumber, and are not always bound by leaf veins.

candidate_sources.title: Downy mildew of cucumber, melon and squash | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Downy mildew is a fungal disease caused by Pseudoperonospora cubensis, which affects cucurbits (cucumber, melon, squash, pumpkin) and a few weeds. This disease is favored by cool (61°-72° F), moist weather. Symptoms appear between 3 and 12 days after infection and begin as slightly yellow to bright yellow lesions on the upper leaf surface.

candidate_sources.title: Diagnostic Guide for Cucurbit Downy Mildew | Plant Health Progress
candidate_sources.snippet: Diagnostic guide describing the cucurbit downy mildew pathogen Pseudoperonospora cubensis, host range including melon and cantaloupe, symptomatology with lesions on cantaloupe having a bright yellow halo and not being angular as in cucumber, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Downy Mildew | Cornell Vegetables
candidate_sources.snippet: Cornell vegetable pathology resource on cucurbit downy mildew caused by Pseudoperonospora cubensis, with symptoms, host range across cucumber, melon, squash and pumpkin, and management recommendations including resistant cultivars and fungicide programs.

candidate_sources.title: Fusarium Wilt (Cantaloupe) / Cucurbits / Agriculture: Pest Management Guidelines / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: Muskmelon wilt is caused by the fungus Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. melonis. Symptoms are typically expressed after fruit set and consist of yellowing of a runner on one side of the plant followed rapidly by wilting of the infected runner. The fungus can attack a susceptible plant at any stage of growth, and infection of the hypocotyl of young plants can result in pre- or post-emergence damping-off. At least four melon races have been reported.

candidate_sources.title: Diseases of Cucurbits: Fusarium Diseases of Cucurbits Fact Sheet (Cornell Cooperative Extension, Page 733.00)
candidate_sources.snippet: Fusarium diseases of cucurbits including Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. melonis on melon, Fusarium solani f. sp. cucurbitae causing crown rot, fruit rot and damping-off. The pathogen attacks only varieties of Cucumis melo. External lesions may develop on roots accompanied by red gumming at or just below the soil surface, and internally, a dark red-brown vascular discoloration extends from roots to runners.

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbits, Fusarium Fruit Rot | UMass Center for Agriculture, Food, and the Environment
candidate_sources.snippet: Fusarium solani f. sp. cucurbitae causes crown and foot rots of summer squash, melon, pumpkin, and a fruit rot of pumpkin. The disease consists of two different races; race 1 targets the stems, roots, and fruit of the plant while race 2 only targets the fruit. Initial infection of the whole plant may appear as damping off during the seedling stage. F. solani f.sp. cucurbitae is borne in and on seed and survives as chlamydospores in the soil for 2-3 years.

candidate_sources.title: Gummy Stem Blight in Cantaloupe | University of Maryland Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Gummy Stem Blight in Cantaloupe (muskmelon) — extension factsheet describing symptoms, pathogen (Stagonosporopsis cucurbitacearum / Didymella bryoniae), favorable conditions and management on melon.

candidate_sources.title: Gummy Stem Blight on Watermelon and Muskmelon | Land-Grant Press | Clemson University
candidate_sources.snippet: Gummy stem blight on watermelon and muskmelon caused by Stagonosporopsis spp. Describes leaf, stem and fruit symptoms including amber-colored gummy exudate, favorable warm wet conditions, and management recommendations.

candidate_sources.title: PP280: Management of Gummy Stem Blight (Black Rot) on Cucurbits in Florida
candidate_sources.snippet: UF/IFAS extension publication on management of gummy stem blight (black rot) caused by Didymella bryoniae / Stagonosporopsis cucurbitacearum on cucurbits including melon — covers symptoms, epidemiology, cultural control, and fungicide programs.

candidate_sources.title: Gummy Stem Blight of Cucurbits | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: NC State Extension publication on gummy stem blight and Phoma blight on cucurbits — pathogen biology (Stagonosporopsis cucurbitacearum, S. citrulli, S. caricae), symptom diagnosis on melon stems, leaves and fruit, and integrated management.

candidate_sources.title: Monosporascus root rot and vine decline of melons
candidate_sources.snippet: Monosporascus root rot and vine decline (MRR/VD), also referred to as sudden wilt, sudden death, melon collapse, Monosporascus wilt, and black pepper root rot, is caused by the root-infecting ascomycete Monosporascus cannonballus, and is characterized by the sudden and generally uniform canopy collapse of entire fields 1-2 weeks prior to harvest. The primary diagnostic sign is the formation of black, spherical, erumpent perithecia on the roots.
diseases_mentioned: Monosporascus root rot and vine decline

candidate_sources.title: Monosporascus Root Rot / Vine Decline - Vegetable Resources (Texas A&M AgriLife)
candidate_sources.snippet: Monosporascus Root Rot / Vine Decline of cucurbits including melon and watermelon. Caused by Monosporascus cannonballus. Discussion of symptoms, pathogen biology, and management practices used in Texas production systems.

candidate_sources.title: Monosporascus Root Rot and Vine Decline - Bayer Vegetables Disease Guide
candidate_sources.snippet: Monosporascus Root Rot and Vine Decline of cucurbit crops including melon. Caused by Monosporascus cannonballus. Symptoms include sudden wilt and collapse near harvest, with characteristic black perithecia on roots. Management through resistant rootstocks, fumigation, and cultural practices.

candidate_sources.title: Monosporascus root rot and vine decline disease of muskmelon: an overview - Tropical Agriculture
candidate_sources.snippet: Review of Monosporascus root rot and vine decline (MRR/VD) of muskmelon caused by Monosporascus cannonballus. Covers symptoms (sudden wilt at fruiting stage, root lesions, vascular browning), pathogen biology, epidemiology, and integrated management.
diseases_mentioned: Monosporascus root rot and vine decline

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora Blight of Cucurbits | Cornell Vegetables
candidate_sources.snippet: Cornell Vegetables factsheet on Phytophthora blight affecting cucurbits including melon, caused by Phytophthora capsici. Covers symptoms (fruit rot, crown rot, leaf blight), pathogen biology, and integrated management.

candidate_sources.title: A Diagnostic Guide for Phytophthora capsici Infecting Vegetable Crops | Plant Health Progress
candidate_sources.snippet: APS Plant Health Progress diagnostic guide for Phytophthora capsici on vegetable crops including melon and other cucurbits. Provides detailed symptom descriptions, host range, and diagnostic information.

candidate_sources.title: Vine Crops Disease: Phytophthora Blight - Wisconsin Horticulture
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Wisconsin Extension factsheet on Phytophthora blight of vine crops (cucurbits including melon). Watch for suddenly wilting plants in wet fields with brown stems at the soil surface and soft, rotting fruit. Caused by Phytophthora capsici which overwinters as oospores.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora Blight of Cucurbits and Peppers | Oklahoma State University
candidate_sources.snippet: Oklahoma State University Extension factsheet covering Phytophthora blight on cucurbits (including melon) and peppers caused by Phytophthora capsici. Includes symptoms, disease cycle, and management recommendations.

candidate_sources.title: Powdery mildew of cucurbits | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Powdery mildew, mainly caused by the fungus Podosphaera xanthii, infects all cucurbits, including muskmelons, squash, cucumbers, gourds, watermelons and pumpkins. Powdery mildew infections favor humid conditions with temperatures around 68-81F.

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbit Powdery Mildew | Cornell Vegetables
candidate_sources.snippet: Cucurbit powdery mildew factsheet from Cornell. Leaves affected by powdery mildew die prematurely which results in fewer fruit and/or fruit of low quality. Managed with resistant varieties and fungicides.

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbits, Powdery Mildew : Vegetable : UMass Amherst CAFE
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms occur on both leaf surfaces, stems, and petioles as white, powdery fungal growth, developing first on older leaves, shaded lower leaves, lower leaf surfaces, and on older bearing plants. Caused by Podosphaera xanthii (previously Sphaerotheca fuliginea).

candidate_sources.title: Cucurbit Powdery Mildew | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Cucurbit powdery mildew caused by Podosphaera xanthii affects melons, cucumbers, squash and other cucurbits. Information on symptoms, pathogen biology, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Powdery Mildew on Watermelon in Florida - UF/IFAS EDIS
candidate_sources.snippet: The fungus Podosphaera xanthii (previously known as Sphaerotheca fuliginea) is the causal agent of powdery mildew on watermelon, an obligate biotroph that can only survive on living host tissue.

candidate_sources.title: Pythium Root and Fruit Rot - USU Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Pythium Fruit Rot can occur on all cucurbits, including cucumber, pumpkin, squash, gourds, melons, and watermelon. Fruit rot caused by Pythium generally starts as small, water soaked lesions on immature or mature fruit near or in contact with the soil. The whole fruit is involved within 72 hours, the epidermis is ruptured, and the fruit collapses. Caused by a number of species of Pythium (water molds). Most severe in soil that is wet for part of the growing season.

candidate_sources.title: Pythium Fruit Rot : Vegetable : Center for Agriculture, Food, and the Environment (CAFE) at UMass Amherst
candidate_sources.snippet: Cottony leak, sometimes called Pythium fruit rot or Pythium cottony leak, is a common disease of cucurbits. Symptoms begin as brown, water-soaked lesions that quickly develop into soft, rotted areas on the part of the fruit that is in direct contact with the soil. During humid conditions, white, cottony pathogen growth may cover the rotted area. Caused by a number of species of Pythium; most common and severe in heavy, poorly drained soils.

candidate_sources.title: A Diagnostic Guide for Pythium Damping-Off and Root and Stem Rot of Cucurbits | Plant Health Progress
candidate_sources.snippet: Cucurbit damping-off and seedling root rot have been ascribed to several Pythium spp., most frequently P. aphanidermatum, P. ultimum, and P. irregulare. Diseases observed in cucumber, melon, watermelon, pumpkin, summer squash, and winter squash. Typical symptoms include few roots, remaining roots white with cortex sloughed off, and the lower hypocotyl having a soft watery rot. Diagnostic guide covers damping-off, root rot, and stem rot of cucurbits.

candidate_sources.title: Cottony Leak in Cucurbits | Mississippi State University Extension Service
candidate_sources.snippet: Cottony leak is a disease of cucurbit crops including melon, watermelon, cucumber, and squash. Pythium species are soilborne pathogens that can survive indefinitely on organic substrates or as thick-walled oospores. Fruit symptoms include water-soaked lesions, rapid soft rot, and profuse white cottony mycelial growth on infected tissue under humid conditions.

candidate_sources.title: First Report of Geotrichum candidum Causing Sour-Rot of Melon in Brazil | Plant Disease
candidate_sources.snippet: Sour-rot symptoms were observed mainly on the top of the fruit, at the floral insertion site. The lesions were dark and eventually progressed to epidermal cracks. Sour-rot is a well-known disease of melon in other countries and can be listed as a potential postharvest disease for melon fruit.

candidate_sources.title: First report of Geotrichum candidum causing sour-rot of melon in Brazil - CABI
candidate_sources.snippet: Report of Geotrichum candidum (synonym Galactomyces candidus) causing sour-rot on melon (Cucumis melo). Sour rot is an important postharvest disease on fruits and vegetables, with G. candidum as the main causal species.

candidate_sources.title: Molecular phylogeny and morphological characterization of the aetiological agent of sour rot on fruits and vegetables in Brazil - Plant Pathology
candidate_sources.snippet: Sour rot is an important postharvest disease on fruits and vegetables in Brazil, with Geotrichum candidum (synonym Galactomyces candidus) reported as the main species causing this disease. The pathogen causes sour rot of stone fruit, melon, tomato, and other fruit.

candidate_sources.title: Geotrichum Sour Rot of Sweetpotato | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Geotrichum sour rot is caused by the ascomycete fungus Geotrichum candidum, a ubiquitous soil-inhabitant with a world-wide distribution disseminated by wind and water. The pathogen is restricted to areas with high water availability; for pathogenesis, plants must be exposed to high temperature, high moisture, and low oxygen, and usually require a wound for infection. No conventional fungicides are labeled for control.
